title: Somatic Experiencing for Solar Plexus Emotions
meta_description: Hey. You're probably reading this because you're in it. That awful, churning, gut-wrenching feeling that feels like it’s going to tear you apart from the i
body:
Hey. You're probably reading this because you're in it. That awful, churning, gut-wrenching feeling that feels like it’s going to tear you apart from the inside. Right? That place in your solar plexus, just below your ribs, that's screaming bloody murder, but there's no sound. It's just pure, raw sensation, pure terror, pure anxiety, pure – something. I've been there. I know that feeling of being absolutely alone with it, especially at 3 AM when the world's asleep and you're wide awake, vibrating with fear. You're not crazy. You're not broken. You're just having an experience that's a hell of a lot more common than anyone talks about.

What the Hell is Happening in Your Solar Plexus?

Look, when we talk about the solar plexus, we're not just talking about anatomy. We're talking about a center of gravity for your emotional life. It's where your personal power lives, your will, your sense of self in the world. And when things go sideways, especially during something like a spiritual emergency or kundalini awakening - whatever you want to call it - that area can feel like it's been set on fire. Or frozen solid. Or both, somehow, simultaneously. It's where you feel that deep, primal fear, that gut instinct, that sense of dread that tells you something is deeply wrong, even if your logical mind can't pinpoint it.

I'll tell you what, for me, it was like a black hole had opened up there. A constant, nauseating pit that sucked all the joy, all the peace, all the normal human feelings right out of me. It was like living with a permanent knot of dread. And no amount of positive thinking, no amount of meditating "through it," no amount of trying to just "let it go" made a damn bit of difference. In fact, sometimes trying to force it made it worse. You know? It's like trying to hold a beach ball underwater - the harder you push, the more violently it springs back up.

This isn't just "stress." This isn't just "anxiety" in the everyday sense. This is often a deep, somatic response to something much bigger. It's your body reacting to an energetic overload, a restructuring, a purging. It's primal. It's intense. And it demands a different kind of attention than what you're probably used to.

The Kundalini Crisis Connection

So yeah, if you're experiencing this, there's a good chance you've stumbled into the territory of spiritual emergency or a kundalini awakening. It's not always pretty, this "awakening" stuff. Sometimes it feels like you're being torn limb from limb. I've seen it. I've lived it. Books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) really nail what it feels like when your psyche is just blown wide open, and all the old patterns, all the old traumas, all the repressed emotions come roaring to the surface. And guess where a lot of that stuff collects? Yep, your gut. Your solar plexus.

Kundalini, when it gets moving, can feel like an electrical current, a fire, a snake unwinding. And when it hits blockages, when it encounters old trauma or deeply held fear, it can create absolute havoc in your system. That solar plexus area can become a battleground. You might feel intense heat, cold, vibrations, pressure, or just that indescribable, crushing anxiety. It's your nervous system trying to process a whole new level of energy, a whole new reality, and it doesn't know how. It's like plugging a supercomputer into an old, frayed electrical outlet. You're gonna get sparks, and maybe a fire.

I remember feeling like my insides were trying to escape my body, and my solar plexus was the epicenter. It was a constant, low-grade hum of terror that would spike into full-blown panic attacks. I thought I was losing my mind, honestly. And nobody around me seemed to get it. They'd say things like, "Just breathe," or "Try to relax," which, bless their hearts, was about as useful as a screen door on a submarine. What I needed wasn't distraction or denial; I needed to understand what was actually happening in my body and how to work with it, not against it.

Why Somatic Experiencing? Because Your Body Holds the Score

Here's the thing: you can't think your way out of a body problem. And these solar plexus sensations? They're body problems, even if their origin is spiritual or energetic. Your intellect can try to make sense of it all day long, but your nervous system is running the show. And your nervous system speaks the language of sensation, not logic. That's where Somatic Experiencing (SE) comes in. It's not about analyzing your past or getting lost in narratives. It's about tuning into the felt sense in your body, allowing it to move, allowing it to discharge.

SE was developed by Peter Levine, who observed that animals in the wild, after a traumatic event, physically shake off the excess energy. They discharge it. Humans, with our big brains and complex social structures, often override that natural impulse. We "tough it out," we "keep a stiff upper lip," we intellectualize, we suppress. And all that undischarged energy, that frozen fight, flight, or freeze response, gets stuck in our bodies. Guess where a big chunk of it gets stuck? Yep, you guessed it - the solar plexus, the gut, the diaphragm. It's where we clench, where we brace, where we hold our breath in fear.

When you're in a spiritual emergency, it's like your system is being asked to process a lifetime of accumulated stress and trauma, all at once. And if you haven't learned how to discharge that energy safely, it can feel like you're literally exploding. SE gives you the tools to gently, slowly, safely release that stored energy. It's not about reliving the trauma; it's about renegotiating the response to it in the present moment, through your body.

The Power of Titration and Pendulation

So, how does SE actually work with that screaming solar plexus? Two big concepts: titration and pendulation. You're not going to just dive headfirst into the abyss of your fear. That's how you get overwhelmed, re-traumatized, and shut down even harder. Instead, you go in small, manageable doses. That's titration. It's like taking a tiny sip of a very strong drink, rather than chugging the whole thing.

Here's what that might look like: you tune into that horrible feeling in your solar plexus. Just for a moment. You notice it. Then, you gently shift your attention to a part of your body that feels neutral or even good. Maybe your hands, your feet, the top of your head. Somewhere that feels calm, even if it's just a tiny bit. You stay there for a bit, letting your nervous system register that sense of safety. Then, you gently shift back to the solar plexus sensation, just for a moment. And then back to the resource. This back-and-forth movement is pendulation.

It's like swinging a pendulum. You move from the intense, uncomfortable sensation to a place of resource and safety, and back again. What this does is it teaches your nervous system that you can tolerate the sensation without being overwhelmed. It builds your capacity to be with discomfort without freaking out completely. It allows your body to slowly, gradually, discharge that trapped energy in tiny, digestible bits, rather than in one massive, terrifying eruption. This is the path to integration, to actually making sense of what's happening to you, rather than just being swept away by it. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) also talks about the need for careful integration. You don't just "get rid" of these energies; you learn to live with them, to channel them.

Practical Steps for Your Screaming Solar Plexus

Okay, so you're feeling that intense solar plexus energy right now. What can you actually do? This isn't a quick fix, but these are things you can start practicing. Be gentle with yourself. You're dealing with powerful stuff.

  1. Find a Safe Space: First, make sure you're physically safe. Lie down or sit in a comfortable, quiet place where you won't be disturbed. You want to feel supported.
  2. Ground Yourself: Feel your feet on the floor, your body on the chair or bed. Notice the contact points. This brings you into the present moment and helps you feel more anchored. You can even imagine roots growing from your feet deep into the earth. It sounds woo-woo, but it works.
  3. Orienting: Look around your room. Notice objects. Name them silently. "Lamp. Wall. Window. Book." This helps bring your prefrontal cortex online, reminding your nervous system that you're in the present, not back in some past threat.
  4. Track Sensation (Titration): Gently bring your attention to your solar plexus. What do you notice? Is it hot, cold, tight, buzzing, empty, full? Don't judge it, just observe. Can you describe it without attaching a story to it? Just the pure sensation. Maybe you can only stay there for a second. That's okay.
  5. Find a Resource (Pendulation): Now, shift your attention to another part of your body that feels neutral or pleasant. Maybe your hand feels warm, or your feet feel solid, or the top of your head feels light. Even if it's just 5% better, go there. Linger there. Notice the difference. How does that part of your body feel?
  6. Go Back and Forth: Gently move your attention back to your solar plexus sensation for a moment. Then back to your resource. Do this slowly, like a pendulum swinging. Don't force anything. Just observe the subtle shifts. You might notice the sensation in your solar plexus changing - maybe it softens slightly, or shifts location, or changes temperature. Or maybe it stays the same. That's okay too. The goal isn't to get rid of it immediately, but to build your capacity to be with it.
  7. Notice "Good" Sensations: Pay attention to any signs of relaxation or discharge. A sigh, a yawn, a rumbling stomach, a tingling sensation, a warmth spreading, a subtle softening. These are signs your nervous system is starting to release trapped energy. Don't try to make them happen; just notice them if they do.
  8. Movement: Sometimes, allowing small, gentle movements can help. A slight sway, a gentle stretch, shaking your hands or feet. Listen to your body's impulse. Animals shake; we can too.
  9. Don't Push: If you feel overwhelmed, stop. Go back to your resource. Ground yourself more. You're not trying to conquer this feeling; you're learning to befriend it, to allow it to move through you safely.

This isn't a one-and-done thing. This is a practice. You're retraining your nervous system. It takes time. But it's worth it. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another good resource that talks about the long haul of this process. It's not a sprint; it's a marathon, and you need tools for the journey.

The Deeper Meaning: Integration, Not Eradication

You're not trying to get rid of these solar plexus sensations forever. That's not how this works. These intense feelings, even the terrifying ones, are messengers. They're telling you something is shifting, something is coming up for release, something is being integrated. In the context of kundalini, it's often the raw, unprocessed energy of your past - your traumas, your fears, your unexpressed anger, your grief - that's being brought to the surface to be seen, felt, and ultimately, released. The solar plexus is often where we store our sense of safety and control, or the lack thereof.

The goal of working with these sensations isn't to erase them, but to learn to tolerate them, to understand their language, and to allow them to complete their natural cycle of release. When you suppress them, they just go underground, waiting to erupt later, usually with more force. When you learn to be with them, to titrate and pendulate, you're teaching your nervous system that it's safe to feel. You're building resilience. You're expanding your window of tolerance.

Honestly, this journey of spiritual awakening, especially when it goes through the fire of kundalini, is the toughest thing you'll ever do. It asks everything of you. It strips away all your defenses, all your illusions. And that solar plexus feeling? It's often the rawest, most exposed part of that process. But by learning to work with it somatically, you're not just surviving; you're actually transforming. You're becoming stronger, more resilient, and more deeply connected to your true self. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) goes into a lot of these deep processes and the importance of integrating everything that comes up.

It's a process of integration, not eradication. You're not getting rid of parts of yourself; you're bringing them back into the fold, allowing them to be seen, felt, and healed. And when that happens, when those deep, primal energies in your solar plexus start to settle, you'll find a new kind of strength there. A strength that's not about forcing or controlling, but about being present, adaptable, and deeply, truly alive. It's a rough road, I know. But you're not alone in it. Keep breathing. Keep feeling. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
